# How DNA methylation affects gene expression

DNA methylation serves as a pivotal epigenetic mechanism that significantly influences gene regulation by recruiting proteins that facilitate gene repression or by hindering transcription factors from attaching to DNA. This process, alongside histone modifications, forms the core of what is gene expression and plays a fundamental role in molecular biology and genetics, impacting development, differentiation, and the onset of various diseases.

Understanding DNA methylation and its effects on gene expression is vital for advancing our knowledge in the fields of health and disease. It not only sheds light on the underlying mechanisms of gene regulation but also opens avenues for research into therapeutic interventions for genetic and epigenetic disorders.

## Mechanisms of DNA methylation

DNA methylation is a sophisticated epigenetic mechanism crucial for regulating gene expression in eukaryotic organisms. This section delves into the intricate processes and enzymes involved in DNA methylation, highlighting its significance in gene regulation.

### DNA methylation process

### Patterns and detection

### Functional implications of DNA methylation

### Epigenetic inheritance and imprinting

### Interaction with histone modifications

This detailed exploration of the mechanisms of DNA methylation underscores its critical role in gene regulation and its complex interplay with other epigenetic factors.

## Impact of DNA methylation on gene expression

DNA methylation significantly alters gene expression, primarily serving as a mechanism for [gene repression, particularly when methylation occurs within gene promoters](https://www.nature.com/scitable/topicpage/the-role-of-methylation-in-gene-expression-1070/). [This epigenetic modification](https://biomodal.com/blog/what-is-epigenetics-a-brief-introduction-into-how-and-why-our-genes-switch-on-and-off/) can lead to the silencing of crucial genes, including tumour suppressor genes in cancer, which underscores its role in disease progression. Additionally, DNA methylation influences the binding of transcription machinery, either inhibiting it or, conversely, creating a conducive landscape for transcription when it interacts with histone modifiers.

The presence of methyl groups typically inhibits gene expression in vivo. Interestingly, [the removal of these methyl groups, for instance through the application of 5-azacytidine, a demethylating agent, can reactivate gene expression](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC372829/), highlighting the dynamic nature of this epigenetic modification. Methylation’s impact extends to the interactions with DNA of both chromatin proteins and specific transcription factors, further influencing gene regulatory processes.

In some contexts, DNA methylation can act as either a repressive or an activation mark, depending on its precise location within the genetic locus. This dual role is particularly evident in genomic imprinting, where DNA methylation plays a pivotal role in the parent-of-origin-specific expression of alleles. [Disruption in this process, known as Loss of Imprinting (LOI), is associated with various disorders, including Beckwith–Wiedemann Syndrome and different forms of cancer](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6147084/). The aberrant methylation of imprinted loci is a common feature in several cancer types, including colon, breast, and liver cancers, amongst others.

Epigenetic processes, including DNA methylation, regulate gene expression without altering the underlying DNA sequence. These modifications are generally stable during cell replication and can be influenced by environmental factors. DNA methylation patterns are also highly tissue-specific and play a crucial role in the regulation of gene expression across different cell types and developmental stages.

Early life experiences, such as social stressors or maternal psychopathology, have been shown to influence DNA methylation patterns significantly. These early influences can alter methylation in critical genes like the glucocorticoid receptor gene in the brain, potentially impacting stress responses and social behaviours later in life. Furthermore, changes in methylation patterns in the placenta due to prenatal maternal stress suggest that DNA methylation might mediate some of the impacts of environmental stress on foetal development.

Research also indicates that familial factors and the broader social environment can affect methylation patterns, which may have implications for health across the lifespan. For instance, twin studies have shown that cohabitation history can alter methylation at sites associated with high heritability and significant regulatory potential. These findings underscore the importance of considering both genetic and environmental factors in the study of epigenetics and gene regulation.

In summary, DNA methylation is a complex and context-dependent mechanism that plays a critical role in gene expression regulation, development, and disease. Its study not only provides insights into fundamental biological processes but also offers potential avenues for therapeutic intervention in genetic and epigenetic disorders.

## DNA methylation, health, and disease

DNA methylation, a critical epigenetic mechanism, plays a significant role in various health outcomes and disease states. This section explores the multifaceted impacts of DNA methylation on health, particularly focusing on its role in development, cognitive functions, and disease predisposition.

### Role in development and disease

### Cognitive functions and mental health

### Disease predisposition and epigenetic changes

### Environmental influences on methylation

### [Methylation and ageing](https://biomodal.com/applications/ageing/)

### Socioeconomic factors and health disparities

### Research and challenges in epigenetic epidemiology

This exploration into the relationship between DNA methylation and health underscores the complexity of epigenetic mechanisms and their significant impact on human health and disease.

## Conclusion

Through an in-depth exploration, our article has elucidated how DNA methylation serves as a crucial epigenetic mechanism that significantly influences gene expression, impacting development, disease onset, and even our cognitive functions and mental health. We have traversed the multifaceted terrain of DNA methylation, from its intricate roles in gene regulation through the addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases and its collaboration with histone modifications, to its [profound implications on health and disease states](https://www.nature.com/articles/npp2012112). This exposition not only underscores the critical nature of DNA methylation in the regulation of gene expression but also highlights the dynamic interplay between our genetics and the environment, which can modulate these epigenetic marks.

As we reflect on these insights, it becomes evident that DNA methylation represents a pivotal factor in the complex orchestration of our biological narratives, from embryonic development to the ageing process, and across various pathological states such as cancer and autoimmune diseases. The implications of these findings stretch far beyond the microscopic, impacting not just the field of molecular biology but also offering [potential pathways for therapeutic interventions](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC372829/) and a deeper understanding of how our environment and lifestyles might sculpt our genetic expression. As research continues to unravel the mystique of DNA methylation and its broader epigenetic context, there remains a hopeful horizon for advancing our knowledge and potentially ameliorating a range of health outcomes through targeted epigenetic modification strategies.

Highlights
----------

•

Epigenetics, a rapidly expanding field of biology, involves the study of changes in gene expression that don't stem from changes in the DNA sequence, but rather from chemical modifications of DNA and its associated proteins.

•

Epigenetic mechanisms can significantly influence gene expression, cell differentiation, tissue development, and susceptibility to diseases.

•

Understanding epigenetic changes is crucial for elucidating the role of environmental and lifestyle factors in health and disease, as well as the intergenerational transmission of phenotypes.

•

Recent studies indicate that epigenetics may play a vital role in a wide range of diseases, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, neurodevelopmental disorders, and neurodegenerative disorders.

•

Epigenetic modifications are potentially reversible, offering promising new therapeutic approaches for treating these diseases using epigenetic modulators.

•

Epigenetics also offers insights into disease pathogenesis and potential biomarkers for disease diagnosis and risk stratification.

•

However, there are potential risks associated with epigenetic interventions, including unintended consequences like adverse drug reactions, developmental abnormalities, and cancer.

•

Rigorous studies are necessary to minimize the risks associated with epigenetic therapies and to develop safe and effective interventions to improve human health.

•

The article provides a synthetic and historical view of the origin of epigenetics and highlights some of the most relevant achievements in the field.

Abstract
--------

Epigenetics is a rapidly growing field of biology that studies the changes in gene expression that are not due to alterations in the DNA sequence but rather the chemical modifications of DNA and its associated proteins. Epigenetic mechanisms can profoundly influence gene expression, cell differentiation, tissue development, and disease susceptibility. Understanding epigenetic changes is essential to elucidate the mechanisms underlying the increasingly recognized role of environmental and lifestyle factors in health and disease and the intergenerational transmission of phenotypes. Recent studies suggest epigenetics may be critical in various diseases, from cardiovascular disease and cancer to neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative disorders. Epigenetic modifications are potentially reversible and could provide new therapeutic avenues for treating these diseases using epigenetic modulators. Moreover, epigenetics provide insight into disease pathogenesis and biomarkers for disease diagnosis and risk stratification. Nevertheless, epigenetic interventions have the potential for unintended consequences and may potentially lead to increased risks of unexpected outcomes, such as adverse drug reactions, developmental abnormalities, and cancer. Therefore, rigorous studies are essential to minimize the risks associated with epigenetic therapies and to develop safe and effective interventions for improving human health. This article provides a synthetic and historical view of the origin of epigenetics and some of the most relevant achievements.

1 The epigenetic foundation
---------------------------

The evidence that DNA was organized in a peculiar structure named "chromatin" and associated with proteins of unknown function was a very early finding. The term "chromatin" was first introduced by the German biologist Walther Flemming in the late 19th century [[1]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

1.

Fazary, A.E. ∙ Ju, Y.H. ∙ Abd-Rabboh, H.S.M.

**How does chromatin package DNA within nucleus and regulate gene expression?**

_Int J Biol Macromol._ 2017; **101**:862-881

[Crossref](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2017.03.165)

[Scopus (11)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_1_2&dbid=137438953472&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=2-s2.0-85017184200)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28366861/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1016%2Fj.ijbiomac.2017.03.165&pmid=28366861)

. He observed thread-like structures within the cell nucleus during cell division and named them "chromatin" due to their ability to be stained by certain dyes. Although Flemming's observations were fundamental to the study of chromatin, the detailed structure and function were unraveled much later through the collective work of numerous scientists in genetics, biochemistry, and molecular biology. For example, the biochemist Albrecht Kossel contributed significantly to understanding chromatin's composition [[2]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

2.

Parseghian, M.H. ∙ Luhrs, K.A.

**Beyond the walls of the nucleus: the role of histones in cellular signaling and innate immunity**

_Biochem Cell Biol._ 2006; **84**:589-604

[Crossref](https://doi.org/10.1139/o06-082)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16936831/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1139%2Fo06-082&pmid=16936831)

. In 1884, Kossel discovered histones, the protein component of chromatin. He also identified the five nitrogenous bases in nucleic acids: adenine, guanine, cytosine, thymine, and uracil. Later, in the early 20th century, Fritz Miescher further explored the chemistry of nucleic acids and histones, adding to the understanding that chromatin was composed of DNA and histones proteins [[3]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

3.

Michael, A.K. ∙ Thoma, N.H.

**Reading the chromatinized genome**

_Cell._ 2021; **184**:3599-3611

[Full Text](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_3_2&dbid=4&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=10.1016%2Fj.cell.2021.05.029&cf=fulltext&site=cell-site)

[Full Text (PDF)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_3_2&dbid=4&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=10.1016%2Fj.cell.2021.05.029&cf=pdf&site=cell-site)

[Scopus (38)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_3_2&dbid=137438953472&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=2-s2.0-85109037870)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34146479/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1016%2Fj.cell.2021.05.029&pmid=34146479)

. However, the precise organization of chromatin was not established until Roger Kornberg and his colleagues discovered the nucleosome structure in the mid-1970s [[4]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

4.

Kornberg, R.D. ∙ Lorch, Y.

**Primary Role of the Nucleosome**

_Mol Cell._ 2020; **79**:371-375

[Full Text](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_4_2&dbid=4&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=10.1016%2Fj.molcel.2020.07.020&cf=fulltext&site=cell-site)

[Full Text (PDF)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_4_2&dbid=4&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=10.1016%2Fj.molcel.2020.07.020&cf=pdf&site=cell-site)

[Scopus (89)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_4_2&dbid=137438953472&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=2-s2.0-85088988792)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32763226/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1016%2Fj.molcel.2020.07.020&pmid=32763226)

.

The very first definition of Epigenetics was "Epigenesis," a term taken from the ancient Greek language meaning "above genes" and coined by the British biologist Conrad H. Waddington in 1942, later transformed into epigenetics: the discipline that is revolutionizing our understanding of gene regulation, heredity, and modern biology and medicine [[5]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

5.

Van Speybroeck, L.

**From epigenesis to epigenetics: the case of C. H. Waddington**

_Ann N Y Acad Sci._ 2002; **981**:61-81

[Crossref](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-6632.2002.tb04912.x)

[Scopus (203)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_5_2&dbid=137438953472&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=2-s2.0-0036965325)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12547674/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1111%2Fj.1749-6632.2002.tb04912.x&pmid=12547674)

. Waddington was interested in understanding how genes interacted with their environment to produce phenotypic traits. Specifically, epigenetics explores changes in gene expression that occur without alterations to the underlying DNA sequence. In other words, it explores the origin of changes in the chromatin structure. More recently, epigenetics extended its interest to RNA and proteins' secondary modifications mediated by the same mechanisms acting on the chromatin. This article delves into the history of epigenetics, from its early beginnings to its modern implications in clinical practice. For the reader's sake, we divided it into short subchapters focusing on the significant findings or applications in which epigenetic mechanisms seem implicated. As a result, the article is far from exhaustive, and we apologize for the many excellent contributors to this field that, for the limitation of space, will not be named.

Several decades were necessary, and some seminal discoveries about the genome structure and function occurred before epigenetics gained momentum. First, the discovery of the double helix by James Watson and Francis Crick (Nobel prize in 1962), who published their groundbreaking findings in 1953, was essential [[6]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

6.

Watson, J.D. ∙ Crick, F.H

**Molecular structure of nucleic acids: a structure for deoxyribose nucleic acid**

Published in Nature, number 4356 April 25, 1953 _Nature._ 1974; **248**:765

[Crossref](https://doi.org/10.1038/248765a0)

[Scopus (57)](https://www.ejinme.com/servlet/linkout?suffix=e_1_5_1_2_6_2&dbid=137438953472&doi=10.1016%2Fj.ejim.2023.05.036&key=2-s2.0-36949061030)

[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/4599080/)

[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?doi=10.1038%2F248765a0&pmid=4599080)

. The double helix comprises two complementary strands of nucleotides, held together by hydrogen bonds between base pairs—adenine with thymine and guanine with cytosine. This discovery revolutionized life science, ultimately revealing the molecular basis of heredity and providing the foundation for modern genetics, biotechnology, and advanced therapies.

The American geneticist Barbara McClintock, almost simultaneously to the double helix, discovered the so-called transposable elements, providing the first evidence of genome plasticity [[7]](https://www.ejinme.com/article/S0953-6205(23)00193-0/fulltext#)

7.

Georgiev, G.P.

**Mobil

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: DNA methylation primarily functions to repress gene expression by adding methyl groups to cytosine bases, thereby preventing transcription factor binding and facilitating gene silencing.
Aberrant DNA methylation patterns are linked to various diseases, including cancer and neurodegenerative disorders, highlighting its role in disease progression and potential diagnostic biomarkers.
Targeting DNA methylation through demethylating agents offers promising therapeutic strategies for reactivating silenced genes, presenting new avenues for treating genetic and epigenetic disorders.
